caveats: a: Occupation evidence is for NAICS 322, not 322230.
a: No source measures current AI deployment or task time in stationery plants.
rho: This is bounded judgment rather than measured five-year implementation evidence.
rho: Physical automation is excluded unless AI is the operative substitution mechanism.

e: Manufacturing revenue is repeat product supply rather than a pure service contract.

s5: No complete denominator-matched control-transfer series was found.

q: Capture likely varies sharply between commodity and customized products.

o: Imports can displace domestic operators even when physical-product demand remains.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product orders under purchase orders, distributor programs, private-label arrangements, and institutional supply contracts; revenue is generally per unit or per production run rather than time-and-materials billing.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive converting operations, hobby-scale makers, firms primarily engaged in printing or wholesale distribution, non-control financing vehicles, and businesses whose operations cannot transfer independently of the owner.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of envelopes, notebooks, pads, filing products, computer paper, and related stationery that repeatedly supply distributors, institutions, printers, retailers, or business customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 322 paper-manufacturing workforce, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in NAICS 322230. In that broader population, 253,800 of 356,500 employees in June 2026 were production and nonsupervisory workers, and 87,050 paper-goods machine operators were employed in 2025. I treat the physical-production dominance as an upper-bound constraint, then assign most exposure to planning, quoting, customer service, scheduling, procurement, quality documentation, and back-office work. The broader subsector includes more process-intensive mills, which could make this proxy understate exposure for smaller stationery converters.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for NAICS 322, not 322230.
No source measures current AI deployment or task time in stationery plants.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted employee roster and task-time study from 10-15 independent NAICS 322230 plants, split between machine/warehouse work and office workflows, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Most labor directly sets up, feeds, monitors, packs, moves, and maintains converting equipment and is not substitutable by language-model software. Meaningful but minority opportunity remains in order intake, artwork/document checks, estimating, purchasing, production planning, inventory exceptions, sales support, and administration.

d5.bridge: The USPS OIG reports that demand for graphic paper used in mail had been steadily declining before the pandemic, while NAICS 322230 also includes notebooks, pads, filing products, and other non-mail stationery. I translate that directional evidence into a five-year constant-price quantity decline for the mixed 322230 basket. Because envelopes and office filing products are especially exposed to digital substitution but education, art, premium notebooks, and mandated paper uses persist, the mail-paper proxy may overstate decline for the full basket.
caveats: The source is directional and centered on graphic paper for mail, not a complete 322230 volume index.
The BLS PPI cannot separate real volume from price without matched shipment-value data.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year quantity shipment data by 322230 product class from Census product statistics, producer shipment records, or major distributor scanner data would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Electronic communication, document storage, billing, and workflows continue to reduce envelopes, forms, and filing supplies. School, creative, premium tactile, compliance, and physical-note use provide a durable floor, making decline more likely than disappearance.

caveats: The provided n is an estimate produced from size-class and margin assumptions, not a verified target list.
Manufacturing revenue is repeat product supply rather than a pure service contract.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the 99 dataset-estimated LMM candidates, including independence, customer recurrence, EBITDA normalization, and owner dependence, would replace this judgment.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is coherent and most independent manufacturers repeatedly supply external customers. Ineligibility is concentrated in captive divisions, heavily owner-dependent niche makers, distressed commodity capacity without transferable customer relationships, and firms whose economics fall outside the lens despite the dataset's margin bridge.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 322230 ($836,637,900) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 322230 ($6,431,058,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $415,282 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 322230); class <5: 69 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,564,391 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $229,183 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,964,032 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $287,731 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,381,818 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $495,436 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 66 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,688,485 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,151,863 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,434,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,509,634 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $183,882,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,938,830 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 99

caveats: Operator requirement is estimated after demand attrition; eliminated paper use belongs in d5.
Imports can displace domestic operators even when physical-product demand remains.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews quantifying shifts to digital self-service, print-on-demand, imports, and vertically integrated supply, together with lost-order reason codes, would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Demand that remains for physical envelopes, notebooks, pads, and filing products still requires accountable conversion, quality, fulfillment, and customer-specification ownership. Software can eliminate some use occasions and automate ordering/design, but it cannot itself supply the remaining physical goods.

caveats: The industry PPI shows realized output prices, not contractual sharing of AI benefits.
Capture likely varies sharply between commodity and customized products.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples, bid histories, gross-margin bridges, price realization after cost changes, and customer concentration for representative plants would establish actual pass-through dynamics.
q.rationale: Unit-priced products and negotiated purchase orders allow initial retention of lower overhead or faster quoting benefits, but distributor leverage, competitive rebids, private-label transparency, and renewal repricing pass part of recurring savings to customers. Differentiated short runs and service-sensitive accounts should retain more than commodity envelopes or standard pads.

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than measured five-year implementation evidence.
Physical automation is excluded unless AI is the operative substitution mechanism.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level before/after implementations showing adoption, exception rates, labor hours saved, and sustained use across estimating, order entry, scheduling, and procurement would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Standard office workflows can adopt document extraction, assisted estimating, scheduling recommendations, and service copilots within five years, but fragmented ERP data, variable customer specifications, physical quality checks, and low IT capacity at smaller plants limit implementation.

caveats: Census provides an owner-age data product but the fetched page does not provide a 322230-specific age distribution.
No complete denominator-matched control-transfer series was found.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, or GF Data transactions tagged to NAICS 322230 over 10 years, plus owner-age and succession-plan data for the candidate universe, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer rate in the mid-teens is plausible for mature private manufacturers, reflecting retirement, strategic consolidation, and financial sales, but not every aging owner sells and many transfers are internal or fail to close.

principal_driver: Repeat order workflows create a practical, if limited, opportunity to automate estimating, order entry, scheduling, procurement, and customer administration.
principal_weakness: Most wages support physical production while the underlying paper-stationery basket faces persistent digital substitution and competitive pass-through.
